extracts.extract: Cloud-based notebooks (Google Colab, Binder, Kaggle, Azure) solve the problem of IT departments that won't let you install software, computer labs with restricted environments, and the need to share data exploration with journalism colleagues who don't have the same setup.
extracts.notes: Surveys multiple platforms with tradeoffs: Colab free+Python, Binder no-login, Kaggle 20GB uploads
extracts.relevance_rationale: Cloud notebooks endure but LLM-integrated environments like Cursor and ChatGPT have shifted how journalists explore data beyond traditional notebook setups

extracts.extract: SQLite stores dates as text, unlike some other relational databases. To treat them as dates, use the date() function. The strftime function will return NULL if you attempt to parse an invalid date. You can use it to run some limited integrity checks on columns with date values.
extracts.notes: strftime-as-validator pattern: SELECT *, strftime('%Y', open_date) AS date_test FROM inspections WHERE date_test IS NULL
extracts.relevance_rationale: SQLite date handling still relevant but LLMs can now generate these queries from natural language, reducing need to memorize syntax

extracts.extract: Use julianday() to convert date strings into numbers so you can subtract them to find the difference between dates. To convert to years, divide by 365.25.
extracts.notes: Pattern: julianday(close_case_date) - julianday(open_date) / 365.25 AS years_open
extracts.relevance_rationale: Date arithmetic in SQL remains useful but LLMs can write these formulas on demand from plain-English descriptions

extracts.extract: When writing nested queries, it's helpful to write the inner query (subquery) first, then build the outer query on top of that. You must give the inner query a name using AS and set it off using parentheses. Use tabs to set off lines of your query so that you can easily read them.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Subquery logic still matters but LLMs can generate nested SQL from natural language, reducing manual query-building effort

extracts.extract: AP DataKit is an open-source command-line tool designed to better structure and manage projects. It makes it easier to standardize and share work among members of your team, and to keep your past projects organized and easily accessible for future reference. Keep all parts of a project cleanly separated: data, code, configuration, documentation.
extracts.notes: Uses Cookiecutter templates; standard dirs: analysis/, config/, data/manual|processed|source, docs/, output/
extracts.relevance_rationale: Project organization principles like separating data, code, config, and docs remain essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Automate data and code syncing to take the guesswork out of storage and backup. Additional plugins can help you manage the storage of flat data files, sync your code to GitLab or GitHub and push your output to data.world for sharing.
extracts.notes: datakit-data for S3, datakit-github/gitlab for VCS, datakit-dworld for publishing
extracts.relevance_rationale: Automated syncing and version control endure but LLM-era tools integrate these workflows more tightly with AI-assisted coding

extracts.extract: When default table-finding in pdfplumber doesn't work on complex PDFs, use explicit_vertical_lines and explicit_horizontal_lines to tell pdfplumber exactly where table boundaries are. Identify header regions first, extract column separators from header cells, then find row separators by locating key entities (like payee names) in a specific column. Feed those coordinates back as explicit lines.
extracts.notes: VERIFY on every page, not just one sample page - 3000-page PDFs are not trivial
extracts.relevance_rationale: PDF extraction with explicit lines remains relevant for complex layouts but LLM-based document understanding can now handle many cases automatically

extracts.extract: To rotate each page 90 degrees before extraction, use pdftk: pdftk file.pdf cat 1-endeast output file_rotated.pdf. Start with pdfplumber docs. Use debug_tablefinder() to visually inspect what pdfplumber detects. Crop the page to isolate regions of interest before attempting table extraction.
extracts.notes: Iterative approach: visualize -> crop -> adjust settings -> re-visualize
extracts.relevance_rationale: Iterative PDF debugging workflow endures in principle but LLM-based PDF extraction tools reduce the manual tuning cycle

extracts.extract: IMPORTANT - THIS IS A DEMO, TO DO THIS FOR REAL YOU'D WANT TO VERIFY THAT THIS WORKED ON EVERY PAGE, NOT JUST THIS ONE. THIS IS FROM A 3000-PAGE PDF, IT'S NOT AS TRIVIAL AS IT SOUNDS.
extracts.notes: Extraction settings tuned on one page may fail on others in large documents
extracts.relevance_rationale: Verification at scale is a timeless principle -- extraction settings must be validated across all pages regardless of the tool used

extracts.extract: Request medical examiner data or physical reports of deaths. Then map to show where. Enrich with data from Census & Bureau of Labor Statistics. And don't stop checking. Request this information from your state department of health. FOIA new hires, system contracts, training, etc. Build a timeline of any failures.
extracts.notes: Covers racial disparities, unemployment, nursing homes, PPP loans
extracts.relevance_rationale: FOIA strategies for medical examiner data, census enrichment, and timeline-building are investigative methods unaffected by LLMs

extracts.extract: Get data from the state court system. Should include fields like: defendant, charged crime, case disposition, convicted crime. Explore the data and ask questions: How many charged with sexual assault are convicted? Do they plead down? Calculate by county/judicial district. Talk to sources: Rape crisis centers, assault victim advocates, state attorney general.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Court data analysis and source cultivation for criminal justice reporting are core investigative skills independent of AI tools

extracts.extract: Procurement data can be the bread and butter of your beat. FOIA from state comptroller all expenditures and/or contracts on COVID-19 purchases. Build database over time to catch any patterns or interesting expenditures.
extracts.relevance_rationale: FOIA for procurement data and longitudinal tracking of government spending are fundamental investigative practices

extracts.extract: Voter registration data - great for contacts and identity verification. Pet license database - people who won't give you their cell number do want to get their dogs back. Property or parcel databases - who owns that land? FOIA logs - what are other people requesting (most are not reporters).
extracts.relevance_rationale: Using public records like voter registration and property databases for sourcing and identity verification remains essential

extracts.extract: A PDF is not a spreadsheet. ALWAYS fight for that spreadsheet. ALWAYS appeal.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Fighting for machine-readable data formats over PDFs remains critical even as PDF extraction improves with AI

extracts.extract: Create your own tracking systems, priority lists and alerts if you don't have an editor holding you to account. For FOIAs, create a system that tracks what you've sent, legal timeline, when to follow up, each time contact was made, and alerts to action. Give yourself cutoff dates for how long you have to dig into something before deciding you can't move forward (for a coding problem, it can be 20 mins, for a story idea, maybe it's a few days, for a data cleaning project, maybe it's 3 weeks).
extracts.relevance_rationale: Self-management systems for FOIA tracking and time management are organizational skills independent of technology

extracts.extract: Never, never, never touch your raw data! Always make copies just in case.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Never modifying raw data is a timeless data preservation principle

extracts.extract: Design a wide network of expert resources that you can call on for advice or data analysis and cultivate those relationships BEFORE you need them. Find an expert and build a relationship of respect. Let them challenge your method. Let them poke holes in your research. Let them ask questions. This will help you defend the decision-making process and write your methodology later.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Building expert networks for methodology review is a human relationship skill unaffected by LLMs

extracts.extract: See whether other journalists in other regions have done similar stories to the one you're doing. Read their stories and methods, and reach out to them for advice. Often people are thrilled to share their lessons learned from a hard project and to know that they're contributing to your work.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Researching prior art and learning from other journalists remains essential regardless of technology

extracts.extract: Don't discount working with startups, NGOs, granting organizations and academic researchers who have an interest in your reporting. Be clear and transparent about roles and define journalistic boundaries early. For more formal agreements, consider signing an MOU making it clear what role you each have and that they do not have say in your story.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Collaboration with NGOs and academics while maintaining editorial independence is a structural practice unchanged by AI

extracts.extract: Keep track of all your steps. You need to be able to defend your work and it can be used in the future for other stories. Try tools like Workbench, Jupyter Notebooks for this. In Excel, consider making separate tabs for each step of work. Keep notes throughout the process of how you're doing everything, which choices you're making, and why, so that you can easily understand and summarize at the end when you're writing your methodology.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Documenting analytical steps and methodology decisions remains critical for reproducibility with or without LLMs

extracts.extract: Argue for an expandable methodology section. Even if only a few people read it, it's the knowledgeable ones who do. Admit what you don't know or couldn't figure out. Don't be ashamed about being unable to see the entire picture - sometimes stories come in stages. Those missing pieces can also become part of the story.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Transparent methodology sections and admitting limitations are journalistic integrity practices that endure

extracts.extract: For an editor who isn't as tech-savvy, make a notebook or Excel doc showing the steps you did so they and others can understand. Transparency is critical. Don't talk down to them, help them understand what you're trying to achieve and why it can take time.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Communicating technical work to non-technical editors remains essential in any newsroom

extracts.extract: It's not just about the software or code: Critical, logical thinking. Basic math. Understanding the context of the data. Verifying the data with ground truth. Good organizational skills. Asking questions. Knowing which tool to use and how to use it. Knowing how to interpret results.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Critical thinking, basic math, context understanding, and verification against ground truth are fundamental skills no AI replaces

extracts.extract: Getting the data: Find downloadable data online. Scrape it. Request via open records laws. Build your own database.
extracts.notes: Four canonical paths to data
extracts.relevance_rationale: The four canonical paths to data remain valid acquisition strategies in the LLM era

extracts.extract: The role of data: Foundational data, Decorative data, Responsive data.
extracts.notes: Three-part taxonomy of how data functions in a story
extracts.relevance_rationale: The taxonomy of data roles in stories is an editorial concept independent of technology

extracts.extract: The editor's role in data projects: Be clear, consistent about goals. Ask what kinds of data are available. Discuss known/suspected problems with the data. Encourage creative thinking, brainstorm solutions. Ask reporters what worries them. Backstop frequently throughout. Do your own research. Discuss methodology. Look at the data with/without your reporter. Challenge the data (not the reporter).
extracts.relevance_rationale: The editors role in backstopping data projects -- setting goals, challenging data, checking methodology -- is unchanged by AI

extracts.extract: 10 questions every editor should ask: Does the data answer our questions? Does it tell us something we didn't think to ask? Where did you find the data? How did you vet/clean the data? How did you calculate those numbers? Are you keeping a data diary? Did you replicate your data work? Could someone else? Have you consulted experts or done a scientific lit review? Do we need a white paper? Can you write a nerd graf/story now? What is the significance of the data?
extracts.notes: Canonical backstopping checklist from Beelman/LaFleur
extracts.relevance_rationale: The 10 editorial questions checklist for data stories is a timeless verification framework

extracts.extract: Data checks: What is the source of the data? How many records should you have/do you have? Is data missing? Is the data within reasonable ranges? Is everything included? Are there consistency issues? Are totals within reasonable bounds? Have you done internal checks? Are there duplicates? Gut check.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data quality checks for completeness, range, duplicates, and consistency remain essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Do my findings make sense? Compared to what? Company XX has done XX terrible thing, but what about other companies in the same industry? Should you report a rate or a raw number? Is your data on the same scale?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Contextualizing findings through comparison, rates vs counts, and appropriate scale is fundamental analytical reasoning

extracts.extract: Other considerations: Are there codes that you don't understand or questions you need answered? Can you go look at one? Ground truth. Is the data a sample with a margin of error? Are changes over time reasonable? How else could you look at the data? Are there lurking variables? With maps -- did you use the right projection? Are you matching data sets? Beware data portals.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Ground truth verification, lurking variables, and proper map projections are analytical considerations no AI eliminates

extracts.extract: Read the methodology. Know the sample size and margin of error. Know the questions. Know who funded the research. If the source won't provide the methodology, that's a red flag. Don't report numbers for which you haven't checked the methodology.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Evaluating methodology, sample size, funding transparency of research data is core journalistic verification

extracts.extract: Best practices: Look at your data. Maintain a consistent universe of cases. Keep a data diary or detailed markdown file. Backup your data (Never work from original). Check against reports. Make sure you're using the right tool. Check with experts from different sides of the issue. Show findings to the targets and experts. Duplicate your work - don't just rerun the same script that could have created an error. Have another person duplicate your work.
extracts.notes: Distinction: replicating via same script is not sufficient - need independent duplication
extracts.relevance_rationale: Independent duplication of analysis by another person goes beyond what automated tools can validate

extracts.extract: A name is not enough. Lots of people have the same name.
extracts.notes: On matching datasets: need more than name alone
extracts.relevance_rationale: The principle that names alone are insufficient for record matching is a timeless data quality insight

extracts.extract: Understand the methodology: It should be logical, make sense. Find similar stories and study what they did. Check with experts from different sides. Find a guide. Use the IRE Resource Center. Understand the process of how data was gathered.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Studying similar stories and consulting diverse experts to validate methodology remains essential

extracts.extract: Other backstops: Ask for a copy of the record layout. Ask for who else may have done similar research. Read that research. Read the documentation or data how-to file. Find the experts. Ask for a detailed description of the work (white paper to nerd box). Seek a second opinion from a trusted colleague. Have a backstop process in place.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Editorial backstopping processes like record layout review and second opinions are organizational practices that persist

extracts.extract: Data diary: For backstop. For story footnoting/lawyering. For staying on track. What you did. How you did it. File names. Copy of code/syntax. Should be clear so that your editor and colleagues can make sense of it. Later you can link back to page or number in fact check. Highlight everything that was derived from data (or docs) and go back and check.
extracts.relevance_rationale: The data diary practice for reproducibility, fact-checking, and legal defense is unchanged by AI tools

extracts.extract: Gathering your own data - Pitfalls: Time and effort, Accuracy concerns, Consistency concerns. Benefits: Totally original data reporting, Control over all aspects of the data. Reminders: Leave time for follow up after collecting your data. If you've built a database, set a cut-off date. Use verification or double entry. Attempt to get ground truth.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Building original databases with verification and ground truth checks are investigative practices that persist

extracts.extract: Workflow: You need to have one. Everyone needs to use it. Make sure all parties are included in the beginning. Project management system. Google doc. Github. Backout scheduling.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Having a shared workflow and project management system for team coordination is an organizational necessity

extracts.extract: Thesis, what is it, how does the data support it. Outline the project and the story/ies. Footnote story memos and drafts (increases accuracy and saves time). Write the nut grafs first. Structure is as important as prose. Avoid number overload - use your strongest finding from your data. Going from your data findings to your story is little different than going from notes/findings to your story.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Story structure, nut graf writing, and avoiding number overload are craft skills unaffected by AI

extracts.extract: Story Structure: Chronology (or reverse chronology) is the easiest to write and for a reader to follow. Warning: don't make the reader jump time. Thematic structure is more complicated to write, but can be effective for the right material. Even within a thematic structure, you can use internal chronology. Characters: Who are they and what themes/points do they illustrate? Data gives you an entire population to choose the best characters and scenes.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Narrative structure choices and using data to select characters are storytelling principles independent of technology

extracts.extract: The Nerd box: Details of what you did, why, and how you did it. Keeps story flowing. Adds transparency. You don't always need one. Sometimes you need something more extensive. Write it before writing story.
extracts.notes: Write methodology before story so findings are clear in your mind
extracts.relevance_rationale: The methodology box as transparency practice and story-planning device remains essential

extracts.extract: Words matter: Making conclusions you can't back up. Rates versus raw numbers. The 'more' dilemma. Percent versus percentage points. False precision: 53.14 percent. Avoid number overload. Adjust money for inflation. When analyzing income or home values, use median rather than average. Keep numbers to graphics.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Number literacy principles like rates vs counts, inflation adjustment, and median vs average are timeless

extracts.extract: Don't forget about the graphics/apps: They can't be an afterthought. Text needs to be edited. Data needs to be rechecked. Need a workflow - that's not just email.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Integrating graphics into editorial workflow rather than treating them as afterthought remains critical

extracts.extract: One of the most common things journalists want to do with a date/time field is to calculate response times of local public safety units. Convert time into seconds by multiplying TIME(HOUR,MINUTE,SECOND) by 86400. To deal with calls that run across midnight, use an IF statement checking whether the receive date differs from the arrive date.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Excel time calculations remain useful but LLMs can now generate these formulas from natural language descriptions

extracts.extract: Text-to-Columns is handy if the column you are trying to split is delimited. But sometimes it won't work and sometimes your data won't be neat and tidy. String functions (LEFT, MID, SEARCH, RIGHT) save the day when Text-to-Columns strips leading zeros or data isn't consistently delimited. These functions are also available in SQL, R and Python with slight syntax variations.
extracts.notes: Text-to-Columns destroys leading zeros; string functions preserve them
extracts.relevance_rationale: String function concepts endure but LLMs can write these data-cleaning formulas, reducing need to memorize syntax

extracts.extract: When splitting names with middle initials: first assess whether you have a consistent pattern. Check whether periods are consistent. Use LEN to measure string length, then test whether the second-to-last byte is a space using MID and IF. Be sure to troll through your data and look for anomalies. Use the Filter feature or run a Pivot Table to look for problem records.
extracts.notes: Check for suffixes (Jr, III) that break patterns; handle manually if few
extracts.relevance_rationale: Name parsing with Excel functions is increasingly handled by LLMs that can parse varied name formats contextually

extracts.extract: IF statements are extremely powerful for assigning categories to data based on certain criteria and for data cleanup functions that require looking for patterns. Use IF functions to rearrange data that comes in report fashion or has problems that make sorting or PivotTables impossible. IF functions need a pattern in order to work.
extracts.notes: Key insight: IF needs a pattern - identify the repeating structure first
extracts.relevance_rationale: IF-statement patterns for data cleanup endure conceptually but LLMs can generate these formulas from descriptions

extracts.extract: Election data often comes with just raw vote totals and no indication of who won. Use a combination of IF, LARGE, MAX, INDEX, MATCH to determine winners and handle ties. LARGE finds the nth-largest value in an array; if the largest equals the second-largest, it's a tie. INDEX returns a value at a row/column intersection. MATCH finds which column holds the maximum.
extracts.notes: Formula: =IF(LARGE(D3:i3,1)>LARGE(d3:i3,2), INDEX(header,1,MATCH(MAX(D3:i3),D3:i3,0)),Tie)
extracts.relevance_rationale: Election data winner-determination formulas are still useful but LLMs can produce these complex Excel formulas on demand

extracts.extract: VLOOKUP requires that the field you're matching on is the farthest left column of the lookup table. If your table is set up differently, use a combination of INDEX and MATCH functions instead. MATCH finds the row number, INDEX returns the value at that position.
extracts.notes: INDEX-MATCH is more flexible than VLOOKUP for non-standard table layouts
extracts.relevance_rationale: INDEX-MATCH is being superseded by XLOOKUP in modern Excel, and LLMs can generate both approaches

extracts.extract: Tableau Reshaper is a tool for normalizing data, whether you plan to use Tableau or not. Make sure the columns you want to convert are on the right side of your spreadsheet and the columns you want attached to each row are on the left side. Make sure headers are presented exactly as you want them in the final data.
extracts.notes: Converts wide data to long format; exports to CSV if exceeds 1M rows
extracts.relevance_rationale: Wide-to-long reshaping concept endures but specific tools like Tableau Reshaper are superseded by pandas/R and LLM-generated code

extracts.extract: You can use Weeknum and Weekday in conjunction with Pivot Tables to display data in a sort of calendar form. This would be useful if you're looking for patterns in your data based on the calendar. Put WeekNum in the Row, WeekDay in the Column and whatever field you want to count or sum in the Data box.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Calendar pattern analysis concept endures but LLMs can generate pivot table configurations from plain-English requests

extracts.extract: You can build hyperlinks with a formula to launch documents directly from Excel. Use HYPERLINK() with concatenation to build paths from file name columns. Store documents in a sub-directory of the folder where the spreadsheet lives. This works for Word documents, other Excel files or PDFs.
extracts.notes: Pattern: =HYPERLINK(.\documents\ & E2, E2) to link spreadsheet rows to source documents
extracts.relevance_rationale: Hyperlink formulas in Excel are tool-specific knowledge that LLMs can generate; the document management concept persists

extracts.extract: In a graph database, the data is either a node, a relationship, or an attribute. Use Cypher commands CREATE, LOAD CSV, and MERGE to build the graph. MERGE prevents duplicate nodes when loading CSV data. Attributes go in curly braces as property key-value pairs. Every query must start with a MATCH and end with a RETURN.
extracts.notes: Applied to political donation networks
extracts.relevance_rationale: Graph database concepts endure but LLMs can generate Cypher queries and help design graph schemas from natural language

extracts.extract: Use fuzzy matching in Cypher with regex: WHERE d.name =~ '.*Bamford.*' for partial match, WHERE d.name =~ '(?i).*Bamford.*' for case-insensitive. Filter relationships by properties like donation value: WHERE t.value >= 100000. Whenever you're writing Cypher, keep the Refcard up as a syntax reference.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Fuzzy matching in Cypher is increasingly replaceable by LLM-powered entity resolution and semantic matching

extracts.extract: An easy place to track down APIs is a government open data portal. Many are built on Socrata which standardizes the API. The API Endpoint returns JSON data. If you aren't sure about JSON, you can convert API contents to CSV using online tools. Use Postman to visually explore and test API calls before writing code.
extracts.relevance_rationale: API fundamentals persist but LLMs can now write API interaction code and even help discover undocumented endpoints

extracts.extract: There are two kinds of API: a web API (talk to a URL) and a Python library API (a way of talking to Python that handles the URL for you behind the scenes). Sometimes it's just easier to write Python than trying to talk to a URL. Use Python requests to download data, then give it to pandas to turn it into a filterable dataframe.
extracts.notes: Example: response = requests.get(url); df = pd.DataFrame(response.json())
extracts.relevance_rationale: Web vs library APIs remain relevant but LLMs can now auto-generate API client code and handle auth flows

extracts.extract: When you use some APIs, you need to prove who you are via API keys. API keys are sometimes in the URL, sometimes a parameter in the URL, sometimes separate keys combined using OAuth, or signed requests with encryption. If you run into OAuth or signed requests, it's best to switch to a library.
extracts.relevance_rationale: API key management endures but LLMs simplify OAuth flows and can generate auth boilerplate

extracts.extract: The best-kept secret of scraping: skipping scraping at all, and using the provider's unadvertised API. If you've ever tried to scrape a site and failed because the URLs didn't seem to work right, look for a secret website API that the site uses internally to load data.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Finding hidden APIs before scraping is a timeless investigative technique that predates and outlasts any tooling era

extracts.extract: Beyond data APIs: Geocoding APIs can convert locations into latitude and longitude (Google Maps, Census Bureau). Communication APIs enable texting or calling (Twilio) or sending emails (Mailgun, Sendgrid). For most APIs they'll offer both a URL-based API and official or unofficial libraries.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Geocoding and communication APIs remain useful but LLM-era tools can orchestrate multi-API workflows conversationally

extracts.extract: For machine learning workshops, use Google Colaboratory which provides both a notebook environment and a GPU for free. Turn on the GPU from Runtime > Change runtime type. You can also load GitHub repos directly into Colab by entering the org name in the GitHub tab.
extracts.notes: Quartz AI Studio workshops for journalists learning ML
extracts.relevance_rationale: Colab still useful for ML but LLM-integrated coding environments have shifted how journalists access GPU compute

extracts.extract: If something seems like too much work, it probably is. Try to find a better solution. Take advantage of being in the IRE/NICAR community - everyone is here to share and help!
extracts.relevance_rationale: Community knowledge-sharing and efficiency instincts are timeless editorial principles

extracts.extract: This project began with a little moment: Reporter Maurice Tamman, who lives on a sailboat in New York Harbor, noticed fish in nearby waters that were normally found farther south. He started digging into the data, and he discovered that marine creatures are fleeing warming seas, in an epic underwater refugee crisis.
extracts.notes: Reuters Ocean Shock investigation - observation leading to data investigation
extracts.relevance_rationale: Noticing anomalies in the physical world as story seeds is a core journalistic instinct unaffected by LLMs

extracts.extract: The Implant Files: Driving this investigation was data collected by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration. The analysis guided reporters to problem devices and helped focus reporting. AP decided to look at spinal cord stimulators. Data gives you an entire population to choose the best characters and scenes.
extracts.notes: Data led to devices, devices led to victims/characters
extracts.relevance_rationale: Using government data to focus investigative reporting is a fundamental CAR practice that endures

extracts.extract: You don't have to be able to do data analyses to be able to understand, manage and edit data-driven stories. But you should have an understanding of its power and limits. You do have to be an organized, logical thinker. Don't be intimidated by your data reporter's skills; have him/her walk you through everything.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Editorial judgment about data power and limits remains essential regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Datedif is useful for calculating ages from birthdates. It gives you the difference between two dates in whatever unit of measure you specify. You can use the TODAY() function to refer to today's date. Example: =Datedif(b2,today(), 'y') gives years since birthdate.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Excel date functions still work but LLMs can generate these formulas from natural language descriptions

extracts.extract: Understanding Excel errors: #DIV/0\! means dividing by zero or blank cell - use IF to check before dividing. #N/A means formula couldn't return a legitimate result, often from VLOOKUP with no match. #NAME? means a typo in your formula. #REF\! means invalid cell reference. #VALUE\! means wrong data type in a function.
extracts.notes: Pattern for DIV/0: =if(b2>0, b2/c2, 0)
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding Excel errors remains useful but LLMs can diagnose and fix formula errors conversationally

extracts.extract: Observable is a JS-based data viz canvas where other cells update in real time as changes are made. You can pull things in from other Observable notebooks. RStudio Cloud provides cloud-based RStudio instances where multiple accounts can participate in a workspace with different roles and modify a project together.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Observable and RStudio Cloud persist but LLM-native data viz tools are reshaping how journalists create visualizations

extracts.extract: The Littlest JupyterHub provides roughly 50-100 users with the same coding environment. Made for folks who are not network admins. You're in control: you specify how users log in, what data they have access to, what resources are available to them. You can set this up on services like Digital Ocean, AWS and others.
extracts.notes: For newsroom or classroom environments needing shared compute
extracts.relevance_rationale: Shared Jupyter environments remain useful but cloud AI platforms now bundle compute, collaboration, and LLM assistance together

extracts.extract: To manage large JSONL files from labeling tasks, use command-line tool json2csv to convert to CSV with selected fields. For large CSV files (3GB+), take a slice with tail -n 5000 to create a manageable working sample. Clean zip files of Mac artifacts with zip -d to remove __MACOSX and .DS_Store entries.
extracts.relevance_rationale: CLI data wrangling skills endure but LLMs can now convert between formats and slice large files conversationally

extracts.extract: Use ClassificationInterpretation.from_learner to identify which images confuse the model most. top_losses() returns the indices of the most confused predictions, allowing you to inspect and fix mislabeled training data.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Model interpretability remains critical but newer frameworks and LLM-assisted debugging simplify error analysis

extracts.extract: Use ffmpeg to extract frames from video at regular intervals: ffmpeg -i source -vf fps=1 -vsync 0 -frame_pts 1 %04d.jpg extracts one frame per second with frame number in filename.
extracts.notes: For building image classification training sets from video
extracts.relevance_rationale: ffmpeg video frame extraction is a durable low-level tool that LLMs cannot replace, only help with syntax

extracts.extract: The goal is to see if we can spot something specific in a video. This would be useful especially if you had hours of video you didn't have time to watch. Our plan will be: Download a computer-vision model pre-trained on 1.2 million images; Further train that model on images containing 'yes bikes' and 'no bikes'; Chop up our video into frames; Use the model to detect bikes in frames
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Sorting Images with fast.ai
extracts.notes: Workflow for applying pre-trained CV model to journalistic video analysis
extracts.relevance_rationale: Video classification workflow endures conceptually but multimodal LLMs can now classify video content directly without custom training

extracts.extract: Training a computer-vision mode from scratch to solve our problem would take thousands of images. Maybe more. Instead we take advantage of an existing model that was trained to detect objects -- from planes, to cars, to dogs, to birds -- by processing millions of images. This model is called 'resnet34.' With fast.ai we can infuse this model with our images (and their labels). This takes advantage of all of resnet34's 'knowledge' of image-detection and tacks on our particular problem. This technique is called 'transfer learning.'
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Transfer Learning with ResNet
extracts.notes: Core concept: reuse existing models rather than training from scratch
extracts.relevance_rationale: Transfer learning concept endures but foundation models and zero-shot LLMs reduce need for fine-tuning on small datasets

extracts.extract: We can take a look to see where the model was most confused, and whether what the model predicted was reasonable or not. In this case the mistakes look reasonable (none of the mistakes seems obviously naive). This is an indicator that our classifier is working correctly. Furthermore, when we plot the confusion matrix, we can see how the mistakes are being made.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Evaluating Model Performance
extracts.notes: Inspecting top losses and confusion matrix as validation step for ML classifiers
extracts.relevance_rationale: Inspecting model confusion and validating predictions remains essential regardless of whether using fast.ai or LLMs

extracts.extract: Probably the trickiest part of machine learning image problems involve pairing the images with their proper labels. There are many ways to do this. Sometimes the labels are in the file names. Sometimes they're in a list. Ours are in two separate folders, or directories. I made these folders by sorting through about 300 maps of helicopter flight paths. Took me about 15 minutes.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Helicopter Circling Detection
extracts.notes: Manual labeling effort is modest -- 300 images in 15 minutes -- for effective ML
extracts.relevance_rationale: The challenge of correctly pairing images with labels is a fundamental ML data quality issue that persists across all eras

extracts.extract: Transformations apply visual fun to the training images to mix things up a bit and help make the model more generalizable -- that is, for example, seeing more kinds of images of bikes than the images we have by changing the lighting, the cropping, the rotation, and the flip of the images.
extracts.notes: Augmentation compensates for small training sets
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data augmentation concepts endure but modern foundation models are less dependent on manual augmentation strategies

extracts.extract: We'll add our new transforms, and we'll change the size of the training images. To the model, these will look like brand new images it has never seen before. That's because in the training process, our convolutional neural network looks at small groups of pixels at a time. If we use bigger versions of the image, it's still a bike to us, but those clusters of pixels will be completely new to the computer.
extracts.notes: Resizing images creates effectively new training data from the same source images
extracts.relevance_rationale: Progressive resizing as a training technique is less critical now that foundation models handle varied input sizes natively

extracts.extract: Working with Dan Keemahill and Madlin Mekelburg over a couple of weeks during the 2019 Texas state legislative session, I have have a set of 3,797 tweets humans at the Austin American-Statesman have determined are -- or are not -- statements that can be fact-checked.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Checkable Tweets Classifier
extracts.notes: Newsroom-academic collaboration for hand-coding training data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Human-labeled tweet classification projects endure conceptually but LLMs can now classify tweets zero-shot without labeled training data

extracts.extract: First, we need a model trained to recognize the patterns of English. We'll be using a model trained on thousands of long Wikipedia articles. It's called wikitext-103. We'll then use transfer learning to further train wikitext-103 on our particular corpus: several thousand #txlege tweets. So we benefit from its training on both English-language articles and #txlege tweets. That will give us a language model that's good at detecting patterns in #txlege tweets.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Language Model Fine-tuning
extracts.notes: Two-step approach: language model then classifier, both using transfer learning
extracts.relevance_rationale: Language model fine-tuning on domain text persists but pre-trained LLMs already understand domain language without wikitext pretraining steps

extracts.extract: We want as many tweets for the language model as possible to learn the patterns of #txlege tweets. We'll start with the text of the 3,797 'hand-coded' tweets (though for the language model, we ignore the checkable/not checkable part of that file). To that, we'll add the additional tweets I collected. We'll mush those all into one big file of tweets.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Building Language Models
extracts.notes: Labeled and unlabeled data combined for language model; labels only needed for classifier
extracts.relevance_rationale: Collecting domain-specific text for model training endures but LLMs can classify tweets without custom language model pretraining

extracts.extract: We can open the 'black box' a little to see what words the model is keying into.
extracts.notes: Using intrinsic attention to inspect which words drive classification decisions
extracts.relevance_rationale: Model interpretability and opening the black box remains essential for journalistic accountability in any AI era

extracts.extract: 'Entities' in documents are, generally, names -- names of people, places, and things such as companies. Finding out which entities are mentioned in a trove of documents can be pretty helpful, especially when you don't previously know someone or some place is included the document. There are services online that do this kind of extraction, including DocumentCloud, Amazon Comprehend and Google Natural Language.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Entity Extraction from Documents
extracts.notes: NER as discovery tool for document dumps -- finding unknown actors
extracts.relevance_rationale: Entity extraction from documents endures as a need but LLMs perform NER far more accurately than spaCy-era pipelines

extracts.extract: JSONL is a file format that stores data in a JSON file, with each record living on its own line in the file. This next block reads the PDF file and turns it into a JSONL file, which is much easier to work with.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: PDF to JSONL Processing
extracts.notes: PDF-to-JSONL as standard preprocessing step for document analysis
extracts.relevance_rationale: PDF-to-structured-data conversion endures but LLMs can now parse PDFs directly without intermediate JSONL pipelines

extracts.extract: We're going to remove 'stop' words, such as a and the. Then we'll remove all the punctuation and pronouns. Plus some garbage tokens that came along with the emails we have. Everything gets cleaned up with this reusable function.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Text Preprocessing for Similarity
extracts.notes: Standard NLP preprocessing pipeline: stop words, punctuation, pronouns, domain-specific garbage
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual stop-word removal and text preprocessing is largely handled automatically by LLM embeddings that understand semantic meaning directly

extracts.extract: Almost every token in the text will have an associated vector of 300 values -- which is, in a way, its position in 300-dimentional space. For a series of words, or an entire page, spaCy averages all the token-vectors into one 300-value vector. We're going to use 'cosine similarity' to compare this one document to every other document in the set.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Document Similarity with Vectors
extracts.notes: Vectors as mathematical representations enabling semantic comparison of documents
extracts.relevance_rationale: Document similarity via word vectors endures conceptually but LLM embeddings are far superior to spaCy 300-dim averaged vectors

extracts.extract: We have to do this because pages and paragraphs often cover multiple topics, which might confuse the model. And, Universal Sentence Encoder is built to encode sentences... and so it ignores anything after the 128th word in its input. The code below cuts the text into sentences, but groups any two consecutive sentences under 15 words long together.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Semantic Search Engine
extracts.notes: Chunking strategy must respect both model input limits and topic coherence
extracts.relevance_rationale: Sentence-level splitting for Universal Sentence Encoder is obsolete as LLMs handle full-document semantic search natively

extracts.extract: When is a sentence 'similar' to another? Remember those 512-dimensional vectors? We're treating two sentences as similar if their vectors are close together. Our search results are 'nearest neighbors,' which is what it sounds like.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Nearest Neighbor Search
extracts.notes: Nearest neighbor as intuitive explanation of semantic search
extracts.relevance_rationale: Nearest-neighbor vector search endures as a concept but LLM-era RAG systems use far more sophisticated retrieval than USE embeddings

extracts.extract: Roughly a third of the content published by Bloomberg News uses some form of automated technology. The system used by the company, Cyborg, is able to assist reporters in churning out thousands of articles on company earnings reports each quarter.
extracts.notes: Scale of automation in major newsroom -- one-third of all content
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom automation at scale endures but LLMs have dramatically expanded what content can be auto-generated beyond structured data

extracts.extract: How do we decide on automation? Is the data available? Can we get it in a format we can use? Once we get the data, is it clean enough to use reliably? Can we process the data regularly? Does anyone care?
extracts.notes: Five-question checklist for deciding whether to automate a reporting task
extracts.relevance_rationale: The decision framework for automation — data availability, reliability, audience need — remains essential editorial judgment

extracts.extract: Automation can track down the what, freeing up the reporter to track down the why. Automation is never going to replace the journalist, it's going to augment the work that they do.
extracts.notes: Key framing: automation as augmentation not replacement
extracts.relevance_rationale: The principle that automation handles the what while reporters pursue the why is a timeless framing that applies even more in the LLM era

extracts.extract: What could go wrong? Document format changes. Incorrect data. Human error. Data not available. Outliers. Something we haven't thought of... until it happens.
extracts.notes: Taxonomy of failure modes for automated data pipelines
extracts.relevance_rationale: Risk awareness for automated systems — format changes, bad data, outliers — is permanently relevant for any automation pipeline

extracts.extract: Improving technologies make it easier to scrape Web sites, pull down data, and produce stories. Automation doesn't have to be done with massive resources and limitless data servers. 'Automate the Boring Stuff With Python.' Web scraping/APIs. Time management skills.
extracts.notes: Automation is accessible to small newsrooms, not just Bloomberg-scale operations
extracts.relevance_rationale: Small-newsroom automation endures but LLMs have dramatically lowered the technical bar, making the Automate the Boring Stuff ethos even easier

extracts.extract: Though Quakebot automatically writes posts and creates maps, posts are never published without human review. Once a post is written the copy desk gets a slack alert and reads through the story before publishing it.
extracts.notes: Human-in-the-loop requirement even for fully automated content
extracts.relevance_rationale: Human review before publishing automated content is an essential editorial safeguard that becomes more important with LLMs

extracts.extract: Quakebot is an application that monitors recent earthquakes, detects 'qualifying' earthquakes, automatically generates post and related assets about the qualifying earthquake, pushes the post into our CMS. Polls the USGS's ANSS GeoJSON feed of world earthquakes. Feed contains all earthquakes in the last hour. Polling frequency is every minute.
extracts.notes: Architecture of automated data monitoring: poll, detect, generate, publish
extracts.relevance_rationale: Quakebot-style structured data pipelines endure but LLMs can now generate richer narrative from the same data feeds

extracts.extract: Our always-on live wildfires map aims to provide our most up-to-date coverage of fire perimeters, hotspots and evacuation zones in California. The map is populated by a custom feed of data sources we assembled and automated. It includes government sources such as CalFire, GeoMAC, NOAA, and NASA. It also mixes in an exclusive feeds of fires spotted by Descartes Labs' satellite imagery algorithm. Each data source has its own python scraper, most of which are available as packages on PyPi.
extracts.notes: Multi-source automated data aggregation combining government and commercial satellite feeds
extracts.relevance_rationale: Live data map architectures endure but LLM-era tools can help generate and maintain geospatial visualizations more efficiently

extracts.extract: In addition to our overall fire map, the app also allows for specific fires to be embedded within articles. This eliminates the need for custom, one-off illustrator or d3 map fire perimeter and evacuation zone maps.
extracts.notes: Automation eliminates one-off production work through reusable embeddable components
extracts.relevance_rationale: Reusable embeddable components that eliminate one-off graphics work is a durable design principle regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Right now we're working on several projects related to the idea of computational story discovery, which is the use of algorithms and automating to surface interesting news leads for journalists. For instance I have some students working on a fact spotting system to scan the congressional record for fact checkable claims and send a daily newsletter.
extracts.notes: Diakopoulos: algorithms as lead-generation tools, not replacement for reporting
extracts.relevance_rationale: Computational story discovery endures as a concept but LLMs can now surface anomalies and leads from data far more flexibly

extracts.extract: We developed this particular system to help support national politics reporters in figuring out where to go to talk to people. What it does is it data mines a national voter file, including every registered voter in the US to try to pull out interesting patterns in things like new registrations as well as other variables of interest. Counties get ranked on the degree of statistical outlier-y-ness as well as factors like the political relevance in terms of vote margins, if the district flipped recently, and if it has an open seat in 2020. A tip sheet is generated using natural language generation to convey the interesting bits from the statistical analysis.
extracts.notes: Diakopoulos Lead Locator: statistical outlier detection + NLG tip sheets for reporters
extracts.relevance_rationale: Voter-file data mining for lead generation endures but LLMs can augment the analysis with richer demographic and sentiment patterns

extracts.extract: The automation here is really limited to the collection and ranking of documents. All of the metadata, summarization, and subsequent reporting around the lead still requires a lot of human effort.
extracts.notes: Automation handles collection and ranking; humans handle interpretation and reporting
extracts.relevance_rationale: The principle that automation handles collection while humans do the reporting remains a foundational insight about AI-journalism boundaries

extracts.extract: Some experts estimate that only about 15% of a reporter's job and about 9% of an editor's job could be automated using current levels of AI technology. More often than not AI and automation technologies actually create new types of work, like the configuration, parameterization, knowledge management, data production, and template writing tasks involved with setting up, operating, and supervising automated systems. Instead of destroying jobs in journalism, AI appears to be creating them.
extracts.notes: Diakopoulos: AI creates new work types rather than replacing existing journalism roles
extracts.relevance_rationale: The 15%/9% automation estimates from pre-LLM era are outdated — LLMs have significantly expanded what can be automated in journalism

extracts.extract: Be honest with yourself. Is this going to take a lot of time? If yes, ask the agency for another file format. But if you're on deadline...
extracts.notes: First step in PDF extraction: assess effort and consider requesting native format
extracts.relevance_rationale: The pragmatic judgment of when to request different file formats vs wrestling with PDFs is a timeless data acquisition instinct

extracts.extract: Try opening the PDF in MS Word. let it figure out where the row and column boundaries are. Then copy and paste to EXCEL. COMETDOCS.com: Free subscription for IRE members, converts in minutes. LAST RESORT: SUBLIME TEXT EDITOR. Copy and paste the PDF contents into Sublime. To ensure the contents can be imported into Google sheets or Excel, we must define where to split the columns. Add a delimiter such as *. Save as TXT or CSV file. Import into Excel.
extracts.notes: Escalation ladder: Word copy-paste, Cometdocs, Sublime text editor as last resort
extracts.relevance_rationale: PDF conversion tools still exist but LLMs can now extract structured data from PDFs directly, reducing reliance on Cometdocs or manual copy-paste

extracts.extract: JOINING PDFS in a FOLDER: pdftk a.pdf b.pdf cat output c.pdf. Or all pdfs in the folder into a new file called combined.pdf: pdftk *.pdf cat output combined.pdf. SPLIT A PDF FILE INTO SEPARATE PAGES: pdftk combined.pdf burst. The files will be automatically named pg_0001.pdf, pg_0002.pdf, etc.
extracts.notes: pdftk commands for joining and splitting PDFs from the command line
extracts.relevance_rationale: pdftk commands endure for bulk PDF manipulation but LLMs can generate these commands and handle more complex PDF workflows

extracts.extract: Why extract text: Searching. Finding and counting entities (names, organizations). Crude count of records. Tabular data. Classifying. Different tools do better at different jobs: DocumentCloud: OCR, entity extraction. Overview: OCR, entity extraction. Cometdocs. Tabula: Text PDFs only, extract tables. PDF Tables. Adobe Acrobat Pro. Abbyy Finereader.
extracts.notes: Tool-to-task mapping for PDF extraction; different tools excel at different jobs
extracts.relevance_rationale: PDF text extraction needs persist but LLMs and multimodal models now handle OCR, entity extraction, and classification in one step

extracts.extract: Why use command-line tools? Free. Reproducible. Lots of online documentation. Many documents, able to run in background (or cloud). Integrate in pipeline with other tools/analysis (like classifier). Understand pieces of process, how other tools work under the hood.
extracts.notes: Case for CLI tools: reproducibility, scale, pipeline integration, understanding
extracts.relevance_rationale: The case for command-line tools — free, reproducible, scriptable, documentable — remains a valid argument for pipeline-oriented work

extracts.extract: A regular expression is a common, compact way to represent some sort of pattern in text. You can use one to: Help you search within a document. Reformat or otherwise clean up an inconsistent or 'dirty' data set. Extract structured data from plain text. Many common programming languages, text editors and search tools support regular expressions, so you can use them in whatever environment you find most comfortable.
extracts.notes: Regex as universal data-cleaning and extraction skill across environments
extracts.relevance_rationale: Regex remains a powerful tool but LLMs can generate regex patterns from natural language and explain existing ones

extracts.extract: Literals: Letters and numbers refer to themselves. Escaping: A ton of characters in regular expressions have special meanings; to override that meaning for a particular character, put a backslash before it. Character classes: You can specify a class of characters by surrounding them in square brackets. Groups: You can surround a part of a regular expression in parentheses to treat it as a unit. Quantifiers: Several symbols exist to let you specify how many times in a row to match a given character, class or group. Anchors: A caret matches the beginning of a line of text. A dollar sign similarly matches the end of a line.
extracts.notes: Core regex building blocks: literals, escaping, classes, groups, quantifiers, anchors
extracts.relevance_rationale: Regex syntax details endure across tools but LLMs can generate and debug regex patterns conversationally

extracts.extract: Scraping is... catching, collecting or coaxing data off the web. Web scrapers are often called CRAWLERS if they pull data from multiple webpages, EXTRACTORS if they pull data from a single webpage.
extracts.notes: Terminology distinction between crawlers (multi-page) and extractors (single-page)
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding the distinction between crawling and extracting is foundational web-data literacy that persists

extracts.extract: You can use this Google Sheets formula to scrape a table or a list: =ImportHTML("url", "element", number of element). The 'url' is the link you are scraping. The 'element' is the HTML tag. The number is the order the element is in.
extracts.notes: No-code scraping with Google Sheets ImportHTML function
extracts.relevance_rationale: Google Sheets import formulas still work but LLM-powered tools can scrape and structure web data more flexibly

extracts.extract: With XML, you can find the 'XPath'. An XPath is like an address, or a map, to a very specific spot in the webpage's code. You can scrape everything at a particular XPath using this Google Sheets formula: =importxml("url","XPath"). XPaths open up a whole new world: all bold text: //b. All large text (known as headers): //h1. All headers with the word 'Parish': //h1[contains(.,'parish')].
extracts.notes: XPath for targeted element extraction via Google Sheets ImportXML
extracts.relevance_rationale: XPath scraping via Google Sheets persists but LLMs can identify and extract data from pages without manual XPath construction

extracts.extract: Google Sheets has a third import function you can use for scraping: =importFeed("source","items"). It scrapes data from an RSS feed, which is useful if you want to scrape something at regular intervals.
extracts.notes: RSS feeds for regular-interval data collection via Google Sheets
extracts.relevance_rationale: RSS feed scraping endures but many feeds have been deprecated and LLM tools can monitor web content changes more broadly

extracts.extract: Some other good tools: DownThemAll (Firefox add-on) highlights a whole list of links or images/media and downloads them. Zapier and IFTTT (websites) -- these automation tools can help keep track of new data, such as new Instagram posts or tweets. Try: save RSS feed content to a Google Sheet or get emailed tweets from a certain area. Web Scraper (Chrome extension) has you build a sitemap for a site to be crawled.
extracts.notes: Ecosystem of no-code/low-code scraping and monitoring tools
extracts.relevance_rationale: Bulk download and automation tools endure but LLM-era browser agents can handle more complex scraping scenarios

extracts.extract: VisiData is an easy-to-use multipurpose tool to explore, clean, edit, and restructure data. Rows can be selected, filtered, and grouped; columns can be rearranged, transformed, and derived via regex or Python expressions; and workflows can be saved, documented, and replayed.
extracts.notes: VisiData as terminal-based Swiss army knife for data exploration and cleaning
extracts.relevance_rationale: VisiData remains a capable terminal tool but LLM-powered data exploration can accomplish similar tasks conversationally

extracts.extract: To persist your customizations from session to session, you can specify them in your computer's ~/.visidatarc file. The ~/.visidatarc file expects its commands to be written in Python. If you know Python and are willing to acquaint yourself with VisiData's architecture, you can use your ~/.visidatarc file to customize VisiData in ways far more powerful than simple option-setting -- including creating your own commands.
extracts.notes: Persistent configuration via .visidatarc for repeatable data analysis workflows
extracts.relevance_rationale: VisiData config customization is tool-specific knowledge that LLMs can generate on demand

extracts.extract: From anywhere within VisiData, you can press D to view the command log relevant to your current sheet. Navigational commands do not appear in the command log. The reason: Navigating around a sheet doesn't alter its contents. The command log behaves virtually the same as any other sheet in VisiData. That means you can sort, filter, and otherwise edit its rows, columns, and cells. That also means you can save just like you'd save any other sheet.
extracts.notes: VisiData command log enables reproducible data analysis workflows
extracts.relevance_rationale: Command logging for reproducibility is a durable principle — tracking analytical steps matters regardless of tool

extracts.extract: To fully replay the example-cmdlog.vd log we saved, run the following command: vd --play example-cmdlog.vd. We don't point VisiData to the dataset itself, since that information (the path to the dataset) is now specified within the command log. You can also run: vd --play example-cmdlog.vd --batch --output cmdlog-results.csv, which will replay the command log in 'batch' mode and save the final sheet to a CSV.
extracts.chapter: VisiData Tutorial: Replay and Batch Processing
extracts.notes: Command log replay enables fully automated batch data processing pipelines
extracts.relevance_rationale: VisiData replay for batch processing is useful but LLM-generated scripts can accomplish repeatable data transformations more flexibly

extracts.extract: VisiData can open a range of filetypes, including: CSV files, and any other delimiter-separated formats. Excel spreadsheets. Fixed-width files. SQLite databases. Postgres databases. JSON / JSONL. XML. Shapefiles. HTML (looks for table tags). Website URLs.
extracts.notes: VisiData handles wide range of formats including databases, geospatial, and web URLs
extracts.relevance_rationale: Multi-format file support is useful but LLMs can read and convert between formats conversationally

extracts.extract: For machine learning, we need access to a computer that's got a lot of memory and can do a lot of math relatively quickly. For the math part, we'll want a computer with a graphics processing unit (GPU). The computer we use can be in the 'cloud'. We've tailored these notebooks for use with Google Colaboratory, which as of this writing is cheapest way to get going (it's currently free). You essentially 'borrow' a virtual computer in the Google universe to do your calculations.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Cloud Computing Setup
extracts.notes: Google Colab as free entry point for GPU-based ML in journalism
extracts.relevance_rationale: GPU access for ML remains relevant but cloud LLM APIs have eliminated the need for local GPU setup for most journalism tasks

extracts.extract: Given a folder full of images, we want a list of descriptions of each image. One way to solve this problem would be to teach, or train a model. For good results, we'd need to use millions of images. It's doable... but there's another option. That is, use a pre-trained model -- one already trained by some other, kind people, who built it using millions of images and then shared it on the internet for others to use. Note that ResNet does not contain 1.2 million images\! It contains the complex mathematical formula -- the neural network and all of its structure and values -- that were fine-tuned by processing those images.
extracts.notes: Key distinction: models contain learned parameters, not training data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Pre-trained model concept endures but modern foundation models go far beyond ImageNet-style classifiers used here

extracts.extract: Instead of downloading and using a pretrained model, we can send each image to an online service to detect the contents. There are several available, and in this case we'll use Google's Vision AI service. These services generally allow you to try some images for free, and then you must pay for any over that limit. As of this writing, Google Vision allows you to do 1,000 checks in a month for free.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Cloud Vision APIs
extracts.notes: Cloud vision APIs as alternative to local models with free tiers for small-scale use
extracts.relevance_rationale: Cloud vision APIs endure but multimodal LLMs now provide richer image understanding than dedicated classification APIs

extracts.extract: To process data in real time -- say, as tweets are tweeted or whenever a helicopter is flying -- you can also host your code on a service like Render. The guide for production deployment to Render is at course.fast.ai/deployment_render.html.
extracts.notes: Moving from notebook prototype to production deployment for real-time classification
extracts.relevance_rationale: Production deployment to services like Render endures but LLM-era tools simplify deployment and add AI capabilities natively

extracts.extract: We want to make a model that will work for our specific, unique problem: detecting whether helicopter flight maps show a copter was 'circling' or 'not circling.' We'll use a pre-trained model -- one already trained by some other, kind people. We'll use the 'smarts' of that model, which has learned to detect patterns in images generally, to solve our particular problem. We do that by additionally training the original model on our own data set -- the helicopter flight maps.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Custom Image Classifier for Journalism
extracts.notes: Adapting general-purpose CV to specific journalistic investigation need
extracts.relevance_rationale: Custom image classifiers via transfer learning persist but multimodal LLMs can solve many classification tasks zero-shot

extracts.extract: We'll be using a very cool, all-purpose video tool called 'ffmpeg.' The next, admittedly cryptic command, pulls out a .jpg image from the video every second (the fps or frames per second is set to 1). It also names the images using the format 'myframe0000.jpg' ... with the four zeros increasing with every frame.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: Video Frame Extraction
extracts.notes: ffmpeg for converting video to frames as preprocessing for image classification
extracts.relevance_rationale: ffmpeg for video frame extraction is a durable low-level utility that LLMs use but do not replace

extracts.extract: Making predictions -- that is, guessing the class or contents of an image -- is something for which you don't need a powerful computer. You don't need a GPU, or graphics processing unit. A regular computer's CPU (central processing unit) will do just fine.
extracts.chapter: Quartz AI Studio: CPU vs GPU for Inference
extracts.notes: Training requires GPU; inference can run on laptop CPU -- key accessibility point
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding that inference is cheap and training is expensive remains a foundational ML concept even in the LLM era

extracts.extract: Our election-result sentences are the brainchild of Aliza Aufrichtig. It is an algorithm that fills in variations in sentences based on notable possible events within voting patterns. You could consider it a madlib, but it takes a lot more programmatic and editorial awareness to get it right in so many cases.
extracts.notes: Template-based automation requires both programming and editorial sophistication
extracts.relevance_rationale: Template-based election sentence generation is now far surpassed by LLMs that can generate natural narrative from structured data

extracts.extract: How do journalists get data? ...from humans? Ask nicely. FOIA. Hard way. ...from computers? Download. Easy way. Scrape. Hard way.
extracts.notes: Simple taxonomy of data acquisition methods for journalists
extracts.relevance_rationale: The taxonomy of data acquisition methods — asking, FOIA, downloading, scraping — is a durable framework for teaching data journalism

extracts.extract: Different programming languages, text editors and other tools handle regular expressions in their own ways. Some of the things that differ most often are: Character classes -- the shorthand for various character classes can vary between environments. Escaping -- some environments have different rules about when certain characters do and don't need to be escaped. Group references -- you might need to refer to groups by using dollar signs rather than backslashes. Flags -- some environments support the use of 'flags', which modify the rules.
extracts.notes: Regex syntax varies across environments -- check character classes, escaping, groups, flags
extracts.relevance_rationale: Regex dialect differences across tools remain real but LLMs can translate between regex flavors and handle edge cases

extracts.extract: Frequency tables are dead-simple, but also quite powerful. For the dead-simple usage: Navigate to any column, and then press Shift-F. With just one keystroke, VisiData has already told us something useful about the dataset: That the 'operators' associated with 31% of the wildlife strikes are, according to the FAA, 'unknown.' We have also learned, from the 'bins' mini-report at the bottom-right of the screen, that there are 282 distinct values in the 'OPERATOR' column.
extracts.notes: Single-keystroke frequency table generation reveals distribution and cardinality instantly
extracts.relevance_rationale: Frequency tables as an analytical concept endure but LLMs can generate them from natural language without memorizing VisiData keystrokes

extracts.extract: Sometimes you want to count how often combinations of columns occur. VisiData also makes this easy. First, turn the columns you want to count into 'key' columns, using the \! button. Then, type gF. With just a few more keystrokes, we've learned something else: Virtually all wildlife strikes with 'unknown' operators were identified based on carcasses found at the airport (rather than reports from pilots, for example).
extracts.notes: Multi-column frequency tables reveal relationships between variables
extracts.relevance_rationale: Multi-column frequency analysis endures conceptually but LLMs can produce cross-tabulations from plain-English requests

extracts.extract: By default, frequency tables just count the number of times each value appears. But in VisiData, you can specify additional calculations by setting the column's 'aggregators'. VisiData's aggregators include min, max, mean, median, sum, distinct, and others. To add an aggregator to a column, navigate to that column and press +. When using aggregators, make sure that you've assigned the proper type (# for integer columns, etc.) to the columns of interest, so that VisiData knows how to calculate the aggregations correctly.
extracts.notes: Must set column types before aggregation; type assignment is a prerequisite for numerical operations
extracts.relevance_rationale: Aggregation concepts endure but LLMs can compute and explain aggregations without tool-specific keystroke knowledge

extracts.extract: To get a bird's-eye view of your entire dataset, press Shift-I, which will provide summary statistics for each of your columns. In VisiData, this is called the 'Describe Sheet'. You'll notice that there are only min/max/median/etc. calculations for the columns we've given types. If we go back to the data sheet and tell VisiData that the HEIGHT, SPEED, and BIRDS_STRUCK fields are numbers, too, then pressing Shift-I will result in full summary statistics.
extracts.notes: Describe Sheet gives instant dataset overview; numeric summaries require explicit type assignment
extracts.relevance_rationale: Summary statistics as a first step endure but LLMs can generate descriptive stats and flag anomalies conversationally

extracts.extract: Rather than guess at your column's data types, VisiData assumes that they're all plain-old text. If that column is a number or date, and you want to do any math-y operations on your column (e.g., sorting, summing, averaging, et cetera), you'll have to specify its type. To set a column's type, navigate over to that column and press one of the following keys: # for Integer, % for Float, $ for Currency, @ for Date, ~ for Text.
extracts.notes: Explicit type assignment is essential before any numerical analysis in VisiData
extracts.relevance_rationale: Column type setting is tool-specific; LLMs auto-infer data types and handle type coercion in generated code

extracts.extract: VisiData provides several ways to filter your datasets: Row selection + double-quote, Frequency tables + Enter, Frequency tables + row selection + g + Enter. Pressing double-quote will create a copy of your current sheet but one that contains only selected rows. From any row in any frequency table, you can press Enter to create a new dataset containing only the rows that match that value.
extracts.notes: Three complementary filtering approaches: select-then-copy, frequency-drill-down, multi-value frequency select
extracts.relevance_rationale: Row filtering concepts endure but LLMs can filter data from natural language descriptions without memorizing tool shortcuts

extracts.extract: In VisiData, you can select rows by evaluating a given Python expression for every row in your dataset. These expressions can reference any column in your dataset. The two keystrokes for this are z| and z\. For instance: z| then OPERATOR == 'BUSINESS' and STATE == 'FL' then Enter selects 625 rows matching that compound condition.
extracts.notes: Python expressions enable complex multi-column filtering without writing scripts
extracts.relevance_rationale: Expression-based row selection endures conceptually but LLMs can generate filter expressions from plain English

extracts.extract: Expression columns evaluate a given Python expression for every row in your dataset. These expressions can reference any column in your dataset. You can create an expression column by pressing =. Let's say we want to identify wildlife strikes that were reported to have occurred at least 100 feet above ground. We could press = to bring up the 'new column expr=' prompt, type HEIGHT >= 100, and press Enter.
extracts.chapter: How to create an expression column
extracts.notes: Expression columns allow on-the-fly derived analysis without modifying source data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Derived columns via expressions endure as a concept but LLMs generate these transformations from natural language

extracts.extract: You can split the text in any column in VisiData into two or more columns, based on a pattern (i.e., a 'regular expression') that you provide. To split a column, navigate to that column, and press :. Each value in the new column is a list of the pieces that resulted from the split. But you probably want each piece in its own column. To do that press (, which is the 'expand column' command.
extracts.chapter: How to create new columns by splitting another one
extracts.notes: Split-then-expand workflow for parsing compound fields like date-time strings
extracts.relevance_rationale: Column splitting by regex endures as a concept but LLMs can parse and restructure column data from natural language descriptions

extracts.extract: Just like you can split a column by using :, you can extract part of a column into a new column by using ;. For instance, if you want to extract the first string of numbers from each aircraft type, (e.g., 28 from PA-28, 46 from PA-46 MALIBU, and 717 from B-717-200), press ; then type (\d+) (with one set of parentheses for each capture group), and then hit Enter.
extracts.chapter: How to create a new column by capturing it from another column
extracts.notes: Regex capture groups allow surgical extraction of structured substrings from messy fields
extracts.relevance_rationale: Regex capture for column extraction endures conceptually but LLMs generate these patterns from plain English

extracts.extract: To join two or more sheets in VisiData, you'll do the following: 1. In the sheets you want to join, mark the shared columns as 'keys'. 2. In the Sheets Sheet, select the sheets you want to join. 3. Press & to open the join-choice prompt, type your desired join type and press Enter. VisiData supports seven types of joins: inner, outer, full, diff, append, extend, merge.
extracts.notes: VisiData supports full range of join types comparable to SQL, all via keyboard shortcuts
extracts.relevance_rationale: Sheet joining via key columns is a durable data concept but LLMs can perform joins from natural language merge requests

extracts.extract: VisiData's pivot tables are similar to pivot tables you might have created in various spreadsheet programs. Pivot tables create a cross-tabulation of two or more columns in a dataset. In VisiData, creating a pivot table involves the following steps: Use \! to designate the column(s) you want to serve as the pivot table's rows. Optional: Use + to specify additional metrics. Navigate to the column you want to serve as the pivot table's columns. Press Shift-W.
extracts.notes: Pivot table creation in 3-4 keystrokes; column order affects output ordering
extracts.relevance_rationale: Pivot tables endure as an analytical concept but LLMs can create cross-tabulations from conversational requests

extracts.extract: To 'melt' a dataset is to reshape it from a 'wide' format to a 'long' one, specifically by converting each value in each column into its own row. Melting a dataset in VisiData involves the following steps: Optional: Use \! to designate the column(s) you want to keep unmelted. Optional: Use - to hide the columns you don't want to appear, at all, in the melted sheet. Press Shift+M.
extracts.notes: Wide-to-long reshaping via melt; key columns stay fixed while non-key columns become variable-value pairs
extracts.relevance_rationale: Wide-to-long reshaping endures as a data concept but LLMs can melt/pivot data from natural language instructions

extracts.extract: If you're only using VisiData to preview a dataset, consider loading just the beginning of the file. If you're working with a simple CSV file, you can accomplish this by using head on the command-line, combined with vd -f csv e.g.: head -n 1000 faa-wildlife-strikes.csv | vd -f csv. Alternatively, you can use a written-for-speed tool, such as xsv, to slice or filter the file before loading it into VisiData. You can also just press Control-c while the data is loading, which will halt the loading process. VisiData handles this gracefully.
extracts.chapter: Only load part of the file
extracts.notes: Three strategies for handling large files: head piping, pre-filtering with xsv, graceful load interruption
extracts.relevance_rationale: Partial file loading for preview endures but LLMs can summarize and explore large datasets without manual head/tail slicing

extracts.extract: By default, VisiData considers the first row of a tabular dataset to be its columns' names. That's a safe assumption for many formats, but Excel spreadsheets often buck that trend, with titles, notes, or other cruft coming before the actual column names.
