You can auto-populate column names by navigating to the row that contains your desired column names, then type g^ to name all unnamed columns with the values in this row, or gz^ to name all columns.
extracts.chapter: How to fix funky spreadsheets column names
extracts.notes: Common Excel import problem: headers not on first row; VisiData offers flexible header row selection
extracts.relevance_rationale: Fixing messy spreadsheet headers is a real-world need but LLMs can detect and fix header issues automatically

extracts.extract: You can do nearly all of the above from the Columns Sheet (Shift-C). When you're dealing with datasets with a particularly large number of columns, doing it this way can often be easier; you can see more of the columns at once, and you can adjust multiple columns at once. Setting column types: You can select multiple rows of the Columns Sheet and type g#, for example, to set all of the selected columns' types to integer.
extracts.chapter: Manipulating columns from the Columns Sheet
extracts.notes: Columns Sheet enables batch type-setting and metadata operations across many columns simultaneously
extracts.relevance_rationale: Bulk column management via metadata sheets is tool-specific; LLMs handle column operations from natural language

extracts.extract: VisiData is fast: it opens in a blink of an eye, and can load multi-megabyte datasets nearly instantly. It's nimble: makes it easy to search, filter, sort, and join any table. It's great for getting a grasp of new datasets: With just a few keystrokes, you can summarize any column. It works with lots of data formats: CSV, Excel, JSON, and more. It's non-destructive: VisiData won't alter your raw data unless you explicitly tell it to.
extracts.notes: Key advantage: non-destructive exploration of data without altering source files
extracts.relevance_rationale: VisiData speed and nimbleness for data exploration endure but LLM-powered tools offer similar rapid exploration conversationally

extracts.extract: VisiData isn't for every task. You might want to choose another tool for: Complex analysis -- you're probably better off using Python, R, or another programming language. 'Literate' analysis -- although VisiData lets you save and replay your analyses, it's not as legible/flexible as Jupyter notebooks, R Markdown, or other tools. Mission-critical analysis -- VisiData is well-engineered, but still a relatively young piece of software.
extracts.notes: VisiData is for exploration, not production analysis; literate/reproducible workflows need different tools
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding tool limitations — when to use programming languages vs exploratory tools — is durable analytical judgment

extracts.extract: Dynamic columns (for instance, those created with the = command) are calculated 'lazily' -- i.e., only when they're needed. If you're running a lot of operations on a dynamic column -- perhaps calculating its average, median, and sum -- you can save some time by first 'caching' it. When you cache a dynamic column, VisiData calculates its current state and never recomputes it. To cache (or re-cache) a column, navigate to it in your sheet and type z'.
extracts.notes: Cache computed columns before running multiple aggregations to avoid repeated recalculation
extracts.relevance_rationale: Column caching for performance is VisiData-specific optimization knowledge that LLMs handle transparently

extracts.extract: For many of the wildlife strikes in the dataset, species is 'unknown'. We want to focus just on the known species, so we'll filter out the unknowns. Press | to raise the select-by-search prompt, type 'unknown', press Enter. Now, all the unknown species are selected. But we want the opposite of that: only known species selected. To do that, let's toggle the selection for all rows, by typing gt (mnemonic: 'global toggle'). Now press double-quote to create a new sheet containing only those rows.
extracts.notes: Select-then-invert pattern: select unwanted rows, toggle all, then copy selected -- effective for excluding categories
extracts.relevance_rationale: Filtering out unknown values to focus analysis is a fundamental data cleaning step that applies in any tool

extracts.extract: To get the denominator for our calculations, we'll want to know the total number of reported collisions for each state. Create a frequency table on the STATE column with Shift-F. Then create a frequency table for the combination of STATE and SPECIES columns using gF. Then join the two frequency tables using the three-step join process with an inner join. Now that we have the numerator and denominator in the same sheet, let's calculate the percentage with an expression column: count * 100 / state_total.
extracts.chapter: Count the number of collisions per state
extracts.notes: Multi-step analytical workflow: create denominator table, create numerator table, join them, compute rate
extracts.relevance_rationale: Computing denominators for rate calculations is foundational statistical reasoning that endures regardless of tool

extracts.extract: Many of the highest-ranking species-state combinations seem to come from 'states' -- like the striped skunk that was struck in Nova Scotia -- with very few reported collisions. So let's limit the results to species-state combinations with at least 20 reports. To do that, we'll use z|, VisiData's 'select by expression' command: count >= 20. Then press double-quote to create a new sheet containing only the selected rows.
extracts.chapter: Limit to rows with at least 20 collisions
extracts.notes: Applying minimum count thresholds to exclude unreliable rates from small samples -- essential for honest analysis
extracts.relevance_rationale: Filtering out small-sample outliers is a durable analytical judgment about statistical reliability

extracts.extract: As you can see, many of the reported collisions are missing height data, or appear to have occurred on the ground (i.e., HEIGHT == 0). So, let's focus only on collisions reported to have occurred above the ground. Type z| and then, at the prompt, type HEIGHT > 0. Then press Enter. Now press double-quote to create a new sheet with only the selected rows.
extracts.chapter: Select only rows where HEIGHT is greater than zero
extracts.notes: Distinguishing between missing data and meaningful zeros is a key analytical decision
extracts.relevance_rationale: Filtering missing or zero values before analysis is a fundamental data quality step that persists

extracts.extract: This is a two-step process. First, navigate to the HEIGHT column, and press + to add an aggregator. At the prompt type 'mean' and then press Enter. Adding an aggregator doesn't change the appearance of the sheet. Then, to get the average for each animal, navigate to the SPECIES column, and press Shift-F to create a frequency sheet. The mean HEIGHT now appears as an additional column in the frequency table.
extracts.chapter: Get the average collision height for each species
extracts.notes: Aggregator-then-frequency pattern: set aggregator on metric column, then create frequency table on grouping column
extracts.relevance_rationale: Aggregation workflow endures conceptually but LLMs can compute means and aggregations from natural language

extracts.extract: What if we want to find the species that are disproportionately involved in collisions in their state? How would you do that? (Hint: It involves just one more frequency table and one more join.)
extracts.notes: Moving from descriptive to comparative analysis: asking about disproportionality requires baseline comparison
extracts.relevance_rationale: Asking about disproportionate representation is a core analytical question that requires human editorial judgment

extracts.extract: From anywhere within VisiData, you can press D to view the command log relevant to your current sheet. It ignores actions you may have taken outside of the current sheet's direct lineage. Essentially, it answers the question: What steps did I take to get here? Navigational commands do not appear in the command log. The reason: Navigating around a sheet doesn't alter its contents.
extracts.notes: Command log provides analytical provenance; distinguishes transformative actions from navigation
extracts.relevance_rationale: Auditing analytical steps via command history is a durable reproducibility principle

extracts.extract: The command log behaves virtually the same as any other sheet in VisiData. That means you can sort, filter, and otherwise edit its rows, columns, and cells. That also means you can save just like you'd save any other sheet, with Control-s. To fully replay a saved command log, run: vd --play example-cmdlog.vd. You can adjust the replay behavior via various command line arguments, such as --replay-wait 2 which will pause two seconds between steps, or --batch --output cmdlog-results.csv which will replay in batch mode and save the result.
extracts.notes: Command logs can be saved, shared, and replayed for reproducible analysis; batch mode enables automation
extracts.relevance_rationale: Saving analytical workflows for reproducibility is a permanent best practice regardless of tool

extracts.extract: VisiData can open a range of filetypes including: CSV files and any other delimiter-separated formats, Excel spreadsheets, Fixed-width files, SQLite databases, Postgres databases, MySQL databases, HDF5 files, SAS files, Stata .dta files, JSON/JSONL, XML, YAML, Shapefiles, HTML (looks for table tags), Website URLs (downloads the HTML, and then looks for table tags).
extracts.notes: VisiData handles nearly all formats journalists encounter; some require additional pip packages
extracts.relevance_rationale: Multi-format file support is useful but LLMs can ingest and convert between all these formats conversationally

extracts.extract: VisiData guesses which kind of file you're trying to open based on the file's extension. For instance, it will try to open my-csv-data.xlsx as an Excel file. To override that default, specify the filetype with the --filetype flag. For instance: vd my-csv-data.xlsx --filetype csv. You can also use the shorter -f flag.
extracts.chapter: How to specify the file format
extracts.notes: Override format detection when file extensions are misleading
extracts.relevance_rationale: File format override flags are tool-specific knowledge that LLMs can provide on demand

extracts.extract: VisiData obeyed but marked some cells with a ! error/warning annotation -- specifically the empty cells, because Python considers it impossible to convert nothingness to a whole number. To examine this general error in greater detail, we can press z + Control-e to reveal its full traceback. There are two types of cell errors: Errors caused by type conversions (yellow exclamation mark) and errors stemming from dynamic column expressions such as =HEIGHT / 0 (red exclamation mark).
extracts.notes: Error annotations reveal data quality issues; two error types distinguish structural from computational problems
extracts.relevance_rationale: Examining cell errors for data quality endures conceptually but LLMs can diagnose and explain data errors automatically

extracts.extract: To persist your customizations from session to session, you can specify them in your computer's ~/.visidatarc file. Open that file in the editor of your choosing and, for each option you wish to modify, write options.my_example_option = my_custom_value. If you know Python and are willing to acquaint yourself with VisiData's architecture, you can use your ~/.visidatarc file to customize VisiData in ways far more powerful than simple option-setting -- including creating your own commands.
extracts.notes: Persistent configuration via .visidatarc enables standardized workflows and custom commands
extracts.relevance_rationale: Config file customization is VisiData-specific; LLMs can generate tool configs from preferences described in natural language

extracts.extract: Installing a plugin involves two steps: First, make the plugin available to VisiData. Depending on the plugin, you'll do this either by saving the plugin file to your ~/.visidata/ directory, or by running pip install [plugin_package_name]. Then, use your ~/.visidatarc configuration file to import the plugin. As with any software you download, plugins may contain malicious code. If you're unsure whether a plugin is safe to install, err on the side of caution.
extracts.notes: Plugin ecosystem extends VisiData capabilities; security caution warranted for third-party code
extracts.relevance_rationale: Plugin installation is VisiData-specific workflow that LLMs can guide users through step by step

extracts.extract: In VisiData, you can create a randomly-sampled copy of any sheet. To create a random-sample sheet, press Space to initiate the longname-command prompt, and type random-rows. At the prompt, type the number of rows you'd like to include, and then press Enter.
extracts.chapter: Select a random sample of rows
extracts.notes: Quick random sampling for exploration of large datasets
extracts.relevance_rationale: Random sampling endures as a technique but LLMs can generate sampling code in any language from natural language

extracts.extract: There's just one step left before we can join the tables: making sure that the two frequency tables share the exact same key column. VisiData uses each sheet's 'key' columns to determine which rows to join. Because the key for the state-counts table is the STATE column, this table should also have STATE as its only key column. That means we need to un-key the SPECIES column.
extracts.chapter: Prepare the state-species frequency table for joining
extracts.notes: Key column alignment is essential before joining; must match key structure between tables being joined
extracts.relevance_rationale: Key column alignment for joins is tool-specific; LLMs handle join logic from natural language merge descriptions

extracts.extract: One of VisiData's strengths is how quickly it lets you summarize your data. Frequency tables are a great example. To create one, press Shift+F. VisiData is an easy-to-use multipurpose tool to explore, clean, edit, and restructure data. Rows can be selected, filtered, and grouped; columns can be rearranged, transformed, and derived via regex or Python expressions; and workflows can be saved, documented, and replayed.
extracts.notes: Terminal-based tools offer speed advantages for initial data exploration over GUI applications
extracts.relevance_rationale: Quick data summarization as a first analytical step is a durable practice regardless of whether done in VisiData or via LLM

extracts.extract: Command logs can help you demonstrate specific VisiData behavior to others, especially if that behavior involves more than a few steps. You'll want to do this, for instance, when filing potential bug reports. Some advice for doing so: When attaching a command log to a GitHub issue or comment, you'll need to change the file extension, since GitHub will reject filenames ending in .vd. If replaying the command log requires a specific dataset, attach that dataset in the same message and aim to make it as simple as possible.
extracts.notes: Command logs serve as shareable analytical provenance; include minimal reproducible datasets when sharing
extracts.relevance_rationale: Sharing reproducible analytical workflows for collaboration and bug reporting is a permanent best practice

extracts.extract: Navigate to any column, and then press Shift-F. With just one keystroke, VisiData has already told us something useful about the dataset: That the 'operators' associated with 31% of the wildlife strikes are, according to the FAA, 'unknown.' We have also learned, from the 'bins' mini-report at the bottom-right of the screen, that there are 282 distinct values in the 'OPERATOR' column.
extracts.notes: Single-keystroke frequency tables for instant column profiling
extracts.relevance_rationale: VisiData frequency table keystrokes are tool-specific; LLMs can produce identical analysis from natural language

extracts.extract: Sometimes you want to count how often combinations of columns occur. VisiData also makes this easy. First, turn the columns you want to count into 'key' columns, using the \! button. Then, type gF. With just a few more keystrokes, we've learned something else: Virtually all wildlife strikes with 'unknown' operators were identified based on carcasses found at the airport (rather than reports from pilots, for example).
extracts.notes: Multi-column frequency tables reveal patterns in variable combinations
extracts.relevance_rationale: Multi-column frequency analysis via VisiData keys is tool-specific; LLMs can cross-tabulate from plain English

Now press double-quote to create a new sheet containing only those rows.
extracts.notes: Select-then-invert pattern for excluding unwanted categories
extracts.relevance_rationale: Filtering out unknown values to focus analysis is a foundational data quality decision that persists across all tools

extracts.extract: Because we'll later be joining this sheet to another sheet, let's hide the percent and histogram columns. There's just one step left before we can join the tables: making sure that the two frequency tables share the exact same key column. VisiData uses each sheet's 'key' columns to determine which rows to join.
extracts.chapter: Prepare the state-species frequency table for joining
extracts.notes: Key column alignment is prerequisite for correct joins
extracts.relevance_rationale: Preparing tables for joining by aligning keys is tool-specific procedure; LLMs handle join alignment automatically

extracts.extract: Now that we have the numerator and denominator in the same sheet, let's calculate the percentage of known-species collisions to each species in each state. Let's create the new column by pressing =, typing count * 100 / state_total, and then pressing Enter. Rename the column, press % to tell VisiData that it's a 'float'-type numeric column. By default, VisiData assumes that newly created columns are just plain-old text.
extracts.notes: Complete workflow: join tables then compute derived metric via expression column
extracts.relevance_rationale: Calculating percentages from numerator/denominator joins is a core analytical reasoning step that persists

extracts.extract: Many of the highest-ranking species-state combinations seem to come from 'states' --- like the striped skunk that was struck in Nova Scotia --- with very few reported collisions. So let's limit the results to species-state combinations with at least 20 reports. To do that, we'll use z|, VisiData's 'select by expression' command. Type z| to raise the selection prompt. Then, type count >= 20.
extracts.chapter: Limit to rows with at least 20 collisions
extracts.notes: Setting minimum count thresholds to avoid misleading rankings from small samples
extracts.relevance_rationale: Filtering small-sample outliers for statistical reliability is durable analytical judgment

extracts.extract: As you can see, many of the reported collisions are missing height data, or appear to have occurred on the ground (i.e., HEIGHT == 0). So, let's focus only on collisions reported to have occurred above the ground. To do that, we'll use z|, VisiData's 'select by expression' command. Type z| and then, at the prompt, type HEIGHT > 0.
extracts.chapter: Select only rows where HEIGHT is greater than zero
extracts.notes: Filtering out missing/zero values before analysis
extracts.relevance_rationale: Filtering zero/missing values before analysis is a fundamental data quality step that persists

extracts.extract: This is a two-step process. First, navigate to the HEIGHT column, and press + to add an aggregator. At the prompt, type mean and then press Enter. Then, to get the average for each animal, navigate to the SPECIES column, and press Shift-F to create a frequency sheet. By default, frequency tables are sorted by the raw count of matching rows. To sort by the highest average collision height, navigate to the HEIGHT_mean column, and press ].
extracts.chapter: Get the average collision height for each species
extracts.notes: Add aggregator to column first, then create frequency table to see grouped stats
extracts.relevance_rationale: Computing mean by group is a durable concept but LLMs produce grouped aggregations from natural language

extracts.extract: It seems like a lot of these species show up just a few times --- too few to be particularly informative. Let's do something about that. First, type z| to raise the 'select by expression' prompt. Then, type count >= 20. Now press double-quote to create a new sheet containing only the selected rows.
extracts.chapter: Limit the results to relatively common species
extracts.notes: Repeated pattern: threshold filtering to ensure analytical reliability
extracts.relevance_rationale: Filtering for minimum sample sizes before drawing conclusions is a durable statistical reasoning practice

extracts.extract: VisiData makes it easy to search, filter, sort, and join any table. It's great for getting a grasp of new datasets. With just a few keystrokes, you can summarize any column. It works with lots of data formats: CSV, Excel, JSON, and more. It's non-destructive: VisiData won't alter your raw data unless you explicitly tell it to.
extracts.notes: Key selling points: speed, format flexibility, non-destructive workflow
extracts.relevance_rationale: VisiData strengths for quick exploration persist but LLM-powered tools now offer similar rapid data summarization

extracts.extract: VisiData isn't for every task. You might want to choose another tool for: Complex analysis --- you're probably better off using Python, R, or another programming language. 'Literate' analysis --- although VisiData lets you save and replay your analyses, it's not as legible/flexible as Jupyter notebooks, R Markdown, or other tools. Mission-critical analysis --- VisiData is well-engineered, but still a relatively young piece of software.
extracts.notes: Honest assessment of tool limitations; reproducibility gap acknowledged
extracts.relevance_rationale: Knowing when to reach for a full programming language vs an exploratory tool is durable analytical judgment

extracts.extract: Puppeteer is Google's Node library that can control headless Chrome. It can: Take screenshots and PDFs of pages, Automate form submission, UI testing, keyboard input, Serve as a testing environment for JavaScript / web page rendering, Scrape web pages (save HTML).
extracts.notes: Headless Chrome automation for scraping JavaScript-rendered pages
extracts.relevance_rationale: Puppeteer for headless browser automation endures but LLM-powered browser agents like Playwright with AI can handle more complex scenarios

extracts.extract: Process for collecting data: Collect --- Just request the page you want, wait for it to load, then save it all. Parse / extract --- At your leisure, load that saved page, and grab the data you seek. Transform --- Take that data and add to a database, save it as JSON, etc.
extracts.notes: Three-phase scraping: collect raw, parse at leisure, transform to usable format
extracts.relevance_rationale: The collect-parse-save scraping pipeline pattern is a durable architectural principle regardless of tool

extracts.extract: Build a pipeline: Amazon Simple Queue Service --- Your to do list of URLs. Amazon Elastic Container Service --- Dockerized tasks are triggered by items in SQS (This is where Puppeteer runs). Amazon Simple Storage Service --- Save all HTML, screenshots and JSON.
extracts.notes: Production-scale scraping architecture: queue-driven, containerized, cloud storage
extracts.relevance_rationale: AWS queue-based scraping pipelines endure but serverless and LLM-era tools simplify distributed scraping architectures

extracts.extract: A note about organization: Use a consistent structure that allows for an easy look at the latest data.
extracts.notes: Brief but important: consistent file naming/structure for scraped data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Consistent file organization for scraped data is a permanent best practice

extracts.extract: Puppeteer Recorder Chrome plugin: Allows you to 'record' your browsing and user interactions, and generates the Puppeteer node you need to reproduce those actions. Record clicks, input events etc. Record screenshots. Pause the recording when navigating. Monitor recorded events. Export to Puppeteer code. Tweak the settings of the generated code.
extracts.chapter: Also helpful: Puppeteer Recorder Chrome plugin
extracts.notes: Record-and-replay approach lowers barrier to writing scraping scripts
extracts.relevance_rationale: Puppeteer Recorder is useful but LLM-powered browser agents can generate scraping code from natural language instructions

extracts.extract: Generally, a web page consists of specifically formatted text files: .html (HyperText Markup Language) files give structure to a page, .css (Cascading Style Sheet) files determine how the page looks, .js (JavaScript) files add interactivity. Most HTML elements are represented by a pair of tags --- an opening tag and a closing tag. HTML elements can have any number of attributes, such as IDs, which uniquely identify elements, classes, which identify a type of element, and styles, which define how specific elements appear --- that will be useful to know about when we're scraping.
extracts.notes: Essential web literacy for identifying scrapeable elements
extracts.relevance_rationale: Understanding HTML/CSS/JS structure is foundational web literacy that persists for anyone working with web data

extracts.extract: You can inspect specific elements on the page by right-clicking on the page and selecting 'Inspect' or 'Inspect Element' from the context menu. Hover over elements in the 'Elements' tab to highlight them on the page. This can be helpful when you're trying to figure how to uniquely identify the element you want to scrape. To examine all of the source code that makes up a page, you can 'view source.' Try to locate the element(s) that you want to target (use Ctrl+F to find).
extracts.notes: Browser DevTools as prerequisite step before writing scraping code
extracts.relevance_rationale: Browser inspection of page elements is a durable skill for understanding web data structure

extracts.extract: There are many ways to grab content from HTML, and every page you scrape data from will require a slightly different trick. At this stage, your job is to find a pattern or identifier in the code for the element you'd like to extract, which we will then give as instructions to our Python code. Inspect the table element or view the page source. How would you pick the table element out from all of the elements that make up the page? Is it the only table? If not, does it have any attributes, like a class or ID, that would allow you to target it?
extracts.notes: Every scrape requires finding unique identifiers -- no universal approach
extracts.relevance_rationale: Finding patterns in HTML to target data extraction is a core scraping skill that endures even with LLM assistance

extracts.extract: Today, we'll use two third-party Python libraries to help us scrape: requests is the de facto standard for making HTTP requests, similar to what happens when you type a URL into a browser window and hit enter. bs4, or BeautifulSoup, is a popular library for parsing HTML into a data structure that Python can work with.
extracts.notes: Core Python scraping stack: requests + BeautifulSoup
extracts.relevance_rationale: requests and BeautifulSoup remain widely used but LLMs can generate complete scraping scripts from page descriptions

extracts.extract: If you want to make sure that your request was successful, you can check the status_code attribute of the Python object that was returned. A 200 code means all is well. 404 means the page wasn't found, etc.
extracts.notes: Always verify HTTP response status before proceeding with parsing
extracts.relevance_rationale: HTTP status code checking for request validation is fundamental web literacy that persists

extracts.extract: Before we start targeting and extracting pieces of data in the HTML, we need to turn that chunk of text into a data structure that Python can work with. That's where the BeautifulSoup (bs4) library comes in. We'll create a new instance of a BeautifulSoup object. We need to give it two things: The HTML we'd like to parse and a string with the name of the type of parser to use --- html.parser is the default and usually fine, but there are other options.
extracts.notes: Parse HTML string into navigable tree structure
extracts.relevance_rationale: BeautifulSoup HTML parsing endures but LLMs can extract data from HTML without manual soup object manipulation

extracts.extract: Our general strategy: 1. Find the main table with the data we want to grab. 2. Get a list of rows (the tr element) in that table. 3. Use a Python for loop to go through each table row and find the data inside it (td, or 'table data'). To accomplish this, we'll use two bs4 methods: find(), which returns the first element that matches whatever criteria you hand it, and find_all(), which returns a list of elements that match the criteria.
extracts.notes: Standard table-scraping pattern: find table, find rows, loop and extract cells
extracts.relevance_rationale: Table row iteration pattern endures but LLMs can generate complete table extraction code from a page description

extracts.extract: Notice that the first item that prints is the header row with the column labels. You are free to keep these headers if you want, but I typically skip that row and define my own list of column names. Another thing to consider: On better-constructed web pages, the cells in the header row will be represented by th ('table header') tags, not td ('table data') tags. We can skip the first row by using list slicing: rows[1:], which means, take everything in the rows list starting with the second item.
extracts.chapter: Loop through the rows and extract the data
extracts.notes: Handle header rows separately; th vs td distinction matters
extracts.relevance_rationale: Header skipping and column naming in scraping endures but LLMs generate these patterns automatically

extracts.extract: This is returning the entire Tag object --- we just want the contents inside it. You can access the .text attribute of the tag to get the text inside. It's also generally good practice to trim off external whitespace for each value, and you can use the Python built-in string method strip() to accomplish this as you march across the row.
extracts.chapter: Loop through the rows and extract the data
extracts.notes: Always strip whitespace when extracting text from HTML elements
extracts.relevance_rationale: Text extraction and whitespace trimming endures but LLMs handle these cleaning steps in generated scraping code automatically

extracts.extract: Now that we've targeted our lists of data for each row, we can use Python's built-in csv module to write each list to a CSV file. Define a list of headers to match the data. Using a with block, open a new CSV file to write to: Create a csv.writer object, Write out the list of headers using the writerow() method, Drop in the for loop and instead of just printing, create a list of items and use writerow() to write your list of data to file.
extracts.notes: Standard pattern for writing scraped data to CSV
extracts.relevance_rationale: CSV writing with Python endures but LLMs generate complete file-output code from natural language

extracts.extract: Remove internal whitespace: Python does not have a built-in string method to remove internal whitespace, but Googling around will yield a common strategy: Using the split() method to separate individual words in the string, then join()ing the resulting list on a single space. As a one-liner: my_text_clean = ' '.join(my_text.split()).
extracts.notes: split-then-join idiom for normalizing internal whitespace
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual internal whitespace removal via split/join is trivial for LLMs to handle in generated code without explicit instruction

extracts.extract: Fetch multiple years: The table we scraped has WARN notices for the current year, but the agency also maintains pages with WARN notices for previous years. See if you can figure out how to loop over multiple pages and scrape the contents of each into a single CSV.
extracts.notes: Scaling from single-page to multi-page scraping via URL iteration
extracts.relevance_rationale: Scraping multiple years by looping over URLs is a durable pattern-recognition and automation skill

extracts.extract: Build a lookup table: Each numeric code in the 'WIA Code' column corresponds to a local area. See if you can figure out how to create a lookup dictionary that maps the numbers to their locations, then as you're looping over the data table, replace the numeric value in that column with the name of the local area instead.
extracts.notes: Dictionary-based lookup for replacing codes with human-readable labels
extracts.relevance_rationale: Building lookup tables from code-to-name mappings endures conceptually but LLMs generate these dictionaries from descriptions

extracts.extract: Fix encoding errors: You might have noticed a few encoding problems --- e.g., Nestle is being rendered as NestlA. This is due to an encoding problem --- the warn_page.text is not encoded as utf-8. Using decode() and encode(), see if you can fix this. Hint: It looks like the state of Maryland is a big fan of latin-1.
extracts.notes: Encoding mismatches common in government web scraping; detect and convert
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual encoding error diagnosis and fix via .encode/.decode is trivially handled by LLMs and modern libraries

extracts.extract: Use Cheerio to parse the HTML file into an object that you can traverse. Extract the table that holds the data we want. Clean up code with regex. Parse the HTML table and quickly turn it into JSON. Save some metadata for the collection. Write the file out.
extracts.notes: Node.js parsing pipeline: Cheerio parse, regex clean, JSON output with metadata
extracts.relevance_rationale: Cheerio HTML parsing and regex cleanup endure but LLMs generate equivalent parsing code from page descriptions

extracts.extract: Dynamic columns (for instance, those created with the = command) are calculated 'lazily' --- i.e., only when they're needed. If you're running a lot of operations on a dynamic column --- perhaps calculating its average, median, and sum --- you can save some time by first 'caching' it. When you cache a dynamic column, VisiData calculates its current state and never recomputes it. To cache a column, navigate to it and type z'.
extracts.notes: Cache computed columns to avoid redundant recalculation
extracts.relevance_rationale: Column caching for performance is VisiData-specific; LLMs handle performance optimization transparently in generated code

extracts.extract: In VisiData, you can create a randomly-sampled copy of any sheet. To create a random-sample sheet, press Space to initiate the longname-command prompt, and type random-rows. At the prompt, type the number of rows you'd like to include, and then press Enter.
extracts.chapter: Select a random sample of rows
extracts.notes: Quick random sampling for large dataset exploration
extracts.relevance_rationale: Random sampling in VisiData is tool-specific; LLMs can generate sampling code in any language from natural language

extracts.extract: You can sort on multiple columns at once by 'key'-ing those columns (via \!) and then typing either g[ (ascending) or g] (descending).
extracts.notes: Multi-column sorting via key columns
extracts.relevance_rationale: Multi-column sorting in VisiData is tool-specific keystroke knowledge that LLMs generate in any tool from English

extracts.extract: You can do nearly all of the above from the Columns Sheet (Shift-C). When you're dealing with datasets with a particularly large number of columns, doing it this way can often be easier; you can see more of the columns at once, and you can adjust multiple columns at once. Setting column types: You can select multiple rows of the Columns Sheet and type g#, for example, to set all of the selected columns' types to integer.
extracts.chapter: Manipulating columns from the Columns Sheet
extracts.notes: Columns Sheet enables batch column operations on wide datasets
extracts.relevance_rationale: Columns Sheet for bulk column management is VisiData-specific; LLMs handle column operations from natural language

extracts.extract: Even if you don't want to edit your raw data in VisiData, knowing how to edit cells will still come in handy, since virtually everything --- including settings --- in VisiData is represented as columns and rows.
extracts.notes: Everything-is-a-sheet philosophy means editing skills transfer to configuration
extracts.relevance_rationale: Cell editing in VisiData is tool-specific; LLMs can guide users through data editing in any tool

extracts.extract: The order of the columns in a pivot table is based on the order the relevant values appear in the source sheet. If you want them to appear, instead, in alphabetical order, sort the source sheet's relevant column(s) first.
extracts.notes: Pivot column order inherits from source; sort first if alphabetical order needed
extracts.relevance_rationale: Pivot table column ordering in VisiData is tool-specific detail that LLMs can advise on when needed
